food_name: Fried Bee Hoon (Hawker Style, Stir-Fried Rice Vermicelli with Egg and Vegetables)
food_subgroup: Stir-fried rice vermicelli with egg, beansprouts, cabbage, carrot, and light soy sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Fried Bee Hoon (hawker-style stir-fried rice vermicelli). Closest parent is a stir-fried rice noodle dish with eggs and vegetables. No exact FoodOn URI can be assigned without fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Stir-fried rice vermicelli with egg, beansprouts, cabbage, carrot, and light soy sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CFA is the closest FoodEx2 parent for stir-fried rice noodle dishes; no exact code exists for this specific Singapore hawker preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of fried bee hoon weighs approximately 300g, as commonly served at Singapore hawker centres and kopitiams.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Bee Hoon (hawker style) — HPB Singapore Foodo / HPB Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for hawker-style fried bee hoon. Values represent the standard Singapore hawker preparation with egg, beansprouts, cabbage, carrot, and light soy sauce.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Rice vermicelli (bee hoon) analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: Rice vermicelli (bee hoon) has a documented GI range of approximately 52–62 in the University of Sydney GI Database. A midpoint value of 57 is applied as the best estimate for this hawker-style preparation. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (57 × 55.5) / 100 = 31.6. The egg, vegetables, and oil in the dish provide a modest GI-buffering effect consistent with mixed-meal methodology.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from light soy sauce and stir-fry seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A standard 300g hawker serving delivers approximately 960mg sodium, representing about 42% of the 2,300mg daily limit; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or choose the plain 'white' version to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 57) and glycemic load (GL 31.6 per 300g serving) from rice vermicelli
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried bee hoon has a moderate GI of 57 and a GL of 31.6 per standard serving, making it a more manageable noodle choice than white rice or fried yellow noodles; portion control to 150–200g and pairing with additional protein can further attenuate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.8g/100g) and zero trans fat with moderate total fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fried bee hoon is relatively low in saturated fat (0.8g/100g, 2.4g per 300g serving) and contains no trans fat, making it one of the more heart-friendly hawker noodle options; however, the 960mg sodium per serving warrants attention for those managing blood pressure as a cardiovascular risk factor.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrients from vegetables and egg
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beansprouts and cabbage contribute vitamin C (5mg/100g) and folate (18mcg DFE/100g), while egg provides selenium (8mcg/100g) and vitamin D (0.2mcg/100g); the low saturated fat content and vegetable-rich composition present a low overall pro-inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Bee Hoon (炒米粉) is one of Singapore's most humble and universally eaten hawker dishes — thin rice vermicelli (bee hoon) soaked and stir-fried in a wok with beaten egg, crunchy beansprouts, shredded cabbage, carrot slivers, and light soy sauce. A simple, economical breakfast or side dish sold at almost every Chinese hawker stall and kopitiam in Singapore. The noodle is light, slightly slippery, and absorbs the wok aroma (wok hei) well. Some versions include small pieces of char siu (BBQ pork) or fish cake for added protein. It can be served plain ('white' version without soy sauce) or with soy seasoning. Nutritionally, fried bee hoon is one of the lighter hawker noodle dishes — lower in fat than fried yellow noodles (mee goreng), with a moderate glycemic index of approximately 57 from the rice vermicelli base, and a decent vegetable content from beansprouts and cabbage. Per 100g it provides 128 kcal, 4.5g protein, 4.5g fat, and 18.5g carbohydrate. A standard 300g hawker plate delivers approximately 384 kcal and 960mg sodium. It is commonly eaten alongside a cup of kopi (local coffee) at breakfast and is suitable for vegetarians and halal diets in its standard egg-and-vegetable form.
common_user_queries: fried bee hoon vs fried noodles calories
fried bee hoon GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. The GI value is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice vermicelli (GI range 52–62), which is the approved GI source. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean dish are present.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this hawker-style preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this dish. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. All core macros (protein_g 4.5, fat_g 4.5, carbohydrate_g 18.5) are non-zero and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (range 52–62); GL per 300g serving = (57 × 55.5) / 100 = 31.6. The 'vegetarian' dietary flag applies to the standard egg-and-vegetable version; versions with char siu or fish cake are not vegetarian.
